questions.scenario: A failure review for Azure Cost and Resilience Mental Model says the first attempted fix did not work. The team changed a nearby resource, but the evidence still points at cost and resilience tradeoffs. What should the next move be?

options.text: Evaluate a different Azure service family if the current service produced the last two incidents.
Move straight to application-code debugging because the symptom is visible to users at the API boundary.
Return to the evidence, confirm the exact failing boundary, and choose the smallest fix that addresses that boundary.
Apply a broader version of the same fix if the first attempt may have been too narrow.

questions.explanation: When a fix fails, the next move is not automatically a bigger fix. The team should return to the evidence and the boundary named by the article, then make the smallest defensible change.

questions.scenario: The team has two possible fixes for a Azure Cost and Resilience Mental Model issue. Fix A is narrow and directly tied to the evidence. Fix B is broader and might cover several unknowns but changes cost shapes, resilience targets, system maps, and review signals. Which choice is safer for a beginner team?

options.text: Choose the fix that makes staging and production match more closely, even if the evidence points to a production-only condition.
Choose the narrow evidence-backed fix first, unless the broader change has a clearly documented reason and rollback plan.
Choose the broader fix because changing more resources reduces the chance of missing a hidden dependency.
Choose the fix that the current on-call can apply fastest, then document any tradeoff after service is stable.

questions.explanation: Narrow, evidence-backed fixes are easier to verify and reverse. Broader changes are sometimes needed, but they deserve explicit reasoning because they increase blast radius.

questions.scenario: Which post-fix checks belong after a change related to Azure Cost and Resilience Mental Model? The review happens during a production change window, so the answer must be based on current Azure evidence rather than a familiar product name or a broad safety guess.

options.text: Re-test the user path or workload behavior that originally failed.
Inspect fresh evidence from tags, resource groups, cost trend, budget alerts, recovery target, redundancy, and restore evidence.
Update the release, incident, or recovery record with the changed resource and reason.
Assume success if the portal shows the new setting, because saved configuration is the same as healthy behavior.

questions.explanation: Saving a setting is not the same as proving the system works. The team should retest behavior, inspect fresh evidence, and update the operating record.

questions.scenario: A rollback plan for Azure Cost and Resilience Mental Model says only, `undo the change if it fails`. The change affects cost shapes, resilience targets, system maps, and review signals. What is missing?

options.text: A clearer release title and owner note, because operators may already know the technical rollback steps.
A dashboard link and alert name, because monitoring can show whether rollback is needed.
A note that the resource is managed by Azure, because managed services reduce some recovery steps.
The rollback target, the exact resources or settings to restore, the evidence that proves rollback worked, and the owner who can make the call.

questions.explanation: Rollback needs concrete targets and proof. Managed services reduce some operations work, but they do not decide which application setting, route, secret, data target, or cost choice should be restored.

questions.scenario: The team wants one final habit from Azure Cost and Resilience Mental Model to carry into future Azure work. Which habit is most useful? The review happens during a production change window, so the answer must be based on current Azure evidence rather than a familiar product name or a broad safety guess.

options.text: Prefer a broader setting temporarily if it reduces repeated approvals, then revisit it during the next review.
Keep the evidence in the incident chat and wait until the monthly review to turn it into a runbook.
Write down the job, the Azure resources involved, the evidence to inspect, the safe fix direction, and the rollback or review path.
Remember the product name and the portal blade where the team fixed it last time.

questions.explanation: The reusable habit is a small operating record. Product names matter, but future operators need job, resources, evidence, fix direction, and rollback or review context.
